Problem Statement¶

Business Context¶

The EdTech industry has been surging in the past decade immensely, and according to a forecast, the Online Education market would be worth $286.62 bn by 2023 with a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 10.26% from 2018 to 2023. The modern era of online education has enforced a lot in its growth and expansion beyond any limit. Due to having many dominant features like ease of information sharing, personalized learning experience, transparency of assessment, etc, it is now preferable to traditional education.

In the present scenario due to the Covid-19, the online education sector has witnessed rapid growth and is attracting a lot of new customers. Due to this rapid growth, many new companies have emerged in this industry. With the availability and ease of use of digital marketing resources, companies can reach out to a wider audience with their offerings. The customers who show interest in these offerings are termed as leads. There are various sources of obtaining leads for Edtech companies, like

  • The customer interacts with the marketing front on social media or other online platforms.
  • The customer browses the website/app and downloads the brochure
  • The customer connects through emails for more information.

The company then nurtures these leads and tries to convert them to paid customers. For this, the representative from the organization connects with the lead on call or through email to share further details.

Objective¶

ExtraaLearn is an initial stage startup that offers programs on cutting-edge technologies to students and professionals to help them upskill/reskill. With a large number of leads being generated on a regular basis, one of the issues faced by ExtraaLearn is to identify which of the leads are more likely to convert so that they can allocate resources accordingly. You, as a data scientist at ExtraaLearn, have been provided the leads data to:

  • Analyze and build an ML model to help identify which leads are more likely to convert to paid customers,
  • Find the factors driving the lead conversion process
  • Create a profile of the leads which are likely to convert

Data Description¶

The data contains the different attributes of leads and their interaction details with ExtraaLearn. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

  • ID: ID of the lead.

  • age: Age of the lead.

  • current_occupation: Current occupation of the lead. Values include 'Professional','Unemployed',and 'Student'.

  • first_interaction: How did the lead first interacted with ExtraaLearn. Values include 'Website', 'Mobile App'.

  • profile_completed: What percentage of profile has been filled by the lead on the website/mobile app. Values include Low - (0-50%), Medium - (50-75%), High (75-100%).

  • website_visits: How many times has a lead visited the website.

  • time_spent_on_website: Total time spent on the website in seconds.

  • page_views_per_visit: Average number of pages on the website viewed during the visits.

  • last_activity: Last interaction between the lead and ExtraaLearn.

    • Email Activity: Seeking for details about program through email, Representative shared information with lead like brochure of program , etc.
    • Phone Activity: Had a Phone Conversation with representative, Had conversation over SMS with representative, etc.
    • Website Activity: Interacted on live chat with representative, Updated profile on website, etc.

  • print_media_type1: Flag indicating whether the lead had seen the ad of ExtraaLearn in the Newspaper.

  • print_media_type2: Flag indicating whether the lead had seen the ad of ExtraaLearn in the Magazine.

  • digital_media: Flag indicating whether the lead had seen the ad of ExtraaLearn on the digital platforms.

  • educational_channels: Flag indicating whether the lead had heard about ExtraaLearn in the education channels like online forums, discussion threads, educational websites, etc.

  • referral: Flag indicating whether the lead had heard about ExtraaLearn through reference.

  • status: Flag indicating whether the lead was converted to a paid customer or not.

Installing and Importing the Necessary libraries¶

In [2]:
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# Libraries to help with reading and manipulating data
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# For splitting the dataset
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# Libaries to help with data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Removes the limit for the number of displayed columns
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)
# Sets the limit for the number of displayed rows
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 100)


# Libraries different ensemble classifiers
from sklearn.ensemble import (
    BaggingClassifier,
    RandomForestClassifier,
    AdaBoostClassifier,
    GradientBoostingClassifier,
)
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier

# Libraries to get different metric scores
from sklearn.metrics import (
    confusion_matrix,
    accuracy_score,
    precision_score,
    recall_score,
    f1_score,
)

# To create the pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer, make_column_transformer
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline,Pipeline


# To tune different models and standardize
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler,OneHotEncoder

# To serialize the model
import joblib

# os related functionalities
import os

# API request
import requests

# for hugging face space authentication to upload files
from huggingface_hub import login, HfApi

Loading the dataset¶

Overview of the dataset¶

In [10]:
# Loading the dataset
data = pd.read_csv("/content/drive/MyDrive/AIML/ExtraaLearn.csv")

print(f"There are {data.shape[0]} rows and {data.shape[1]} columns in the dataset.")

print("Number of duplicate rows:", data.duplicated().sum())

print("Target variable distribution:")
display(pd.DataFrame({
    "Count": data["status"].value_counts().sort_index(),
    "Percentage": (data["status"].value_counts(normalize=True).sort_index() * 100).round(2),
}))

Observations

  • The dataset contains 4,612 rows and 15 columns.
  • There are no missing values in any column, so imputation is not required.
  • There are no duplicate rows, which means the data appears clean at the row level.
  • The ID column has 4,612 unique values, so it is only an identifier and should likely be dropped before modeling.
  • The dataset has 5 numerical columns and 10 categorical/object columns.
  • The target variable status is binary, with:
    • 0: 3,235 records or 70.14%
    • 1: 1,377 records or 29.86%
  • This shows a moderate class imbalance, so model evaluation should not rely only on accuracy.
  • The average age is about 46 years, with ages ranging from 18 to 63.
  • Most users are Professionals, making up the largest occupation group.
  • Most first interactions happened through the Website rather than the Mobile App.
  • website_visits, time_spent_on_website, and page_views_per_visit have high maximum values, suggesting possible right-skewness or outliers.
  • Most marketing channel columns such as print_media_type1, print_media_type2, digital_media, educational_channels, and referral are dominated by No, meaning fewer users came through these channels.

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

The below functions need to be defined to carry out the EDA.

In [11]:
def histogram_boxplot(data, feature, figsize=(15, 10), kde=False, bins=None):
    """
    Boxplot and histogram combined

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    figsize: size of figure (default (15,10))
    kde: whether to show the density curve (default False)
    bins: number of bins for histogram (default None)
    """
    f2, (ax_box2, ax_hist2) = plt.subplots(
        nrows=2,  # Number of rows of the subplot grid= 2
        sharex=True,  # x-axis will be shared among all subplots
        gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": (0.25, 0.75)},
        figsize=figsize,
    )  # creating the 2 subplots
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data, x=feature, ax=ax_box2, showmeans=True, color="violet"
    )  # boxplot will be created and a triangle will indicate the mean value of the column
    sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2, bins=bins
    ) if bins else sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2
    )  # For histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].mean(), color="green", linestyle="--"
    )  # Add mean to the histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].median(), color="black", linestyle="-"
    )  # Add median to the histogram
In [12]:
# function to create labeled barplots


def labeled_barplot(data, feature, perc=False, n=None):
    """
    Barplot with percentage at the top

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    perc: whether to display percentages instead of count (default is False)
    n: displays the top n category levels (default is None, i.e., display all levels)
    """

    total = len(data[feature])  # length of the column
    count = data[feature].nunique()
    if n is None:
        plt.figure(figsize=(count + 2, 6))
    else:
        plt.figure(figsize=(n + 2, 6))

    plt.xticks(rotation=90, fontsize=15)
    ax = sns.countplot(
        data=data,
        x=feature,
        order=data[feature].value_counts().index[:n],
    )

    for p in ax.patches:
        if perc == True:
            label = "{:.1f}%".format(
                100 * p.get_height() / total
            )  # percentage of each class of the category
        else:
            label = p.get_height()  # count of each level of the category

        x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2  # width of the plot
        y = p.get_height()  # height of the plot

        ax.annotate(
            label,
            (x, y),
            ha="center",
            va="center",
            size=12,
            xytext=(0, 5),
            textcoords="offset points",
        )  # annotate the percentage

    plt.show()  # show the plot
In [13]:
def stacked_barplot(data, predictor, target):
    """
    Print the category counts and plot a stacked bar chart

    data: dataframe
    predictor: independent variable
    target: target variable
    """
    count = data[predictor].nunique()
    sorter = data[target].value_counts().index[-1]
    tab1 = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], margins=True).sort_values(
        by=sorter, ascending=False
    )
    print(tab1)
    print("-" * 120)
    tab = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], normalize="index").sort_values(
        by=sorter, ascending=False
    )
    tab.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True, figsize=(count + 5, 5))
    plt.legend(
        loc="lower left", frameon=False,
    )
    plt.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1, 1))
    plt.show()
In [14]:
### function to plot distributions wrt target


def distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, predictor, target):

    fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10))

    target_uniq = data[target].unique()

    axs[0, 0].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[0]))
    sns.histplot(
        data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[0]],
        x=predictor,
        kde=True,
        ax=axs[0, 0],
        color="teal",
        stat="density",
    )

    axs[0, 1].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[1]))
    sns.histplot(
        data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[1]],
        x=predictor,
        kde=True,
        ax=axs[0, 1],
        color="orange",
        stat="density",
    )

    axs[1, 0].set_title("Boxplot w.r.t target")
    sns.boxplot(data=data, x=target, y=predictor, ax=axs[1, 0])

    axs[1, 1].set_title("Boxplot (without outliers) w.r.t target")
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data,
        x=target,
        y=predictor,
        ax=axs[1, 1],
        showfliers=False,
    )

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Univariate Analysis¶

In [15]:
numerical_columns = ["age", "website_visits", "time_spent_on_website", "page_views_per_visit"]
categorical_columns = [
    "current_occupation",
    "first_interaction",
    "profile_completed",
    "last_activity",
    "print_media_type1",
    "print_media_type2",
    "digital_media",
    "educational_channels",
    "referral",
]

for column in numerical_columns:
    histogram_boxplot(data, column, kde=True)

for column in categorical_columns + ["status"]:
    labeled_barplot(data, column, perc=True)

Observations From Age Distribution

  • The age variable ranges from about 18 to 63 years.
  • Most individuals are concentrated in the mid-50s to early-60s age range.
  • The mean age is around 46, while the median is around 51, meaning the distribution is left-skewed.
  • The boxplot shows that the middle 50% of ages lie roughly between 36 and 57 years.
  • There are no clear outliers in the age column.
  • The histogram suggests the age distribution is not normally distributed, with a strong concentration among older individuals and fewer younger individuals.

Observations From Website Visits

  • The website_visits variable ranges from 0 to 30 visits.
  • Most users visited the website only a few times, mainly between 1 and 5 visits.
  • The distribution is strongly right-skewed, with a long tail toward higher visit counts.
  • The mean is higher than the median, indicating that a small number of users with many visits are pulling the average upward.
  • The boxplot shows several high-value outliers, especially above roughly 9 visits.
  • Very few users visited the website more than 10 times.
  • Since the feature is skewed and contains outliers, it may benefit from transformation or outlier treatment before modeling.

Observations From Time Spent on Website

  • time_spent_on_website ranges from 0 to about 2,537 seconds.
  • Most users spend relatively low time on the website, especially between 0 and 500 seconds.
  • The distribution is right-skewed, with a long tail toward users who spend much more time.
  • The mean is greater than the median, showing that high-time users are pulling the average upward.
  • The boxplot shows a wide spread, meaning user engagement time varies strongly across visitors.
  • There are no clear extreme outliers, but there are many high-duration values.
  • This feature may be useful for predicting status, since higher time spent can indicate stronger user engagement.

Observations From Page Views Per Visit

  • page_views_per_visit ranges from 0 to about 18.4 pages.
  • Most users view around 2 to 4 pages per visit.
  • The distribution is right-skewed, with a long tail toward very high page views.
  • The mean is slightly higher than the median, suggesting that high page-view users pull the average upward.
  • The boxplot shows many upper-end outliers, especially beyond roughly 6 page views per visit.
  • There are also some users with 0 page views, which may represent very short visits, tracking issues, or inactive sessions.
  • This feature likely reflects engagement level and may be useful for predicting status.

Observations From Categorical Variables

  • Most individuals are Professionals at 56.7%, followed by Unemployed at 31.2% and Students at 12.0%.
  • The first interaction channel is fairly balanced, but Website is slightly higher at 55.1% compared to Mobile App at 44.9%.
  • Most users have either High or Medium profile completion, at 49.1% and 48.6% respectively. Very few users have Low profile completion, only 2.3%.
  • For last_activity, Email Activity is the most common at 49.4%, followed by Phone Activity at 26.8% and Website Activity at 23.9%.
  • Most users were not reached through digital media, with 88.6% No and only 11.4% Yes.
  • Most users were also not reached through educational channels, with 84.7% No and 15.3% Yes.
  • Referrals are very rare: 98.0% No and only 2.0% Yes.
  • The target variable status is imbalanced: 70.1% belong to class 0, while 29.9% belong to class 1.
  • Overall, the dataset is dominated by professionals, website-based first interactions, high/medium profile completion, and non-referral/non-media sources.

Bivariate Analysis¶

In [16]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.heatmap(data[numerical_columns + ["status"]].corr(), annot=True, cmap="YlGnBu", fmt=".2f")
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap")
plt.show()

for column in numerical_columns:
    distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, column, "status")

for column in categorical_columns:
    stacked_barplot(data, column, "status")

conversion_by_category = []
for column in categorical_columns:
    temp = (
        data.groupby(column)["status"]
        .agg(Count="count", Conversion_Rate="mean")
        .reset_index()
    )
    temp["Feature"] = column
    temp["Conversion_Rate"] = (temp["Conversion_Rate"] * 100).round(2)
    conversion_by_category.append(temp[["Feature", column, "Count", "Conversion_Rate"]])

for table in conversion_by_category:
    display(table.sort_values("Conversion_Rate", ascending=False))

Bivariate Observations With Target

  • time_spent_on_website has the strongest numerical relationship with status, with a correlation of about 0.30. Users who converted generally spent much more time on the website.
  • age has a weak positive relationship with status at about 0.12. Converted users appear slightly older on average.
  • website_visits and page_views_per_visit show almost no linear correlation with status, so visit count alone may not strongly separate converters from non-converters.
  • The numerical features have low correlations with each other, so there is no major multicollinearity concern among them.

Categorical Observations

  • first_interaction is highly important: users whose first interaction was through the Website converted at 45.59%, compared to only 10.53% for Mobile App.
  • profile_completed shows a clear pattern: High profile completion has the highest conversion rate at 41.78%, followed by Medium at 18.88%, and Low at 7.48%.
  • last_activity also matters. Website Activity has the highest conversion rate at 38.45%, followed by Email Activity at 30.33%, and Phone Activity at 21.31%.
  • referral has a very high conversion rate: 67.74% for referred users versus 29.08% for non-referred users. However, only 93 users came through referrals, so this group is small.
  • print_media_type1, print_media_type2, and digital_media show only small differences in conversion rates, so they may have weaker predictive power.
  • educational_channels does not appear to improve conversion; users marked Yes have a slightly lower conversion rate than those marked No.

Overall, the strongest predictors appear to be first interaction channel, profile completion level, time spent on website, last activity, and referral.

Data Preprocessing¶

Outlier Check¶

In [17]:
outlier_summary = []
for column in numerical_columns:
    q1 = data[column].quantile(0.25)
    q3 = data[column].quantile(0.75)
    iqr = q3 - q1
    lower_bound = q1 - 1.5 * iqr
    upper_bound = q3 + 1.5 * iqr
    outlier_count = data[(data[column] < lower_bound) | (data[column] > upper_bound)].shape[0]
    outlier_summary.append(
        {
            "Feature": column,
            "Lower Bound": lower_bound,
            "Upper Bound": upper_bound,
            "Outlier Count": outlier_count,
            "Outlier %": round(outlier_count / data.shape[0] * 100, 2),
        }
    )

outlier_summary = pd.DataFrame(outlier_summary)
display(outlier_summary)

for column in numerical_columns:
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 3))
    sns.boxplot(data=data, x=column)
    plt.title(f"Outlier Check - {column}")
    plt.show()

# The selected tree-based models are robust to outliers, so no outlier capping is applied.
Feature Lower Bound Upper Bound Outlier Count Outlier %
0 age 4.50 88.500 0 0.00
1 website_visits -2.50 9.500 154 3.34
2 time_spent_on_website -1633.25 3118.750 0 0.00
3 page_views_per_visit -0.44 6.274 257 5.57

Outlier Observations

  • age has no outliers, with all values falling within the acceptable range of 4.50 to 88.50.
  • time_spent_on_website also has no outliers, based on the calculated bounds of -1633.25 to 3118.75.
  • website_visits has 154 outliers, representing 3.34% of the dataset. These are users with unusually high numbers of visits, above the upper bound of 9.50.
  • page_views_per_visit has 257 outliers, representing 5.57% of the dataset. These are users with unusually high page views per visit, above the upper bound of 6.274.
  • Since the outlier percentages are relatively small, especially below 6%, they may not need to be removed immediately.
  • The outliers in website_visits and page_views_per_visit could represent highly engaged users, so they may contain useful business information.
  • For modeling, it may be better to use outlier treatment such as capping/winsorization rather than deleting these records.

Data Preparation for modeling¶

  • We want to predict which lead is more likely to be converted.
  • Before we proceed to build a model, we'll have to encode categorical features.
  • We'll split the data into train and test to be able to evaluate the model that we build on the train data.
In [18]:
X = data.drop(["ID", "status"], axis=1)
y = data["status"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X,
    y,
    test_size=0.30,
    random_state=1,
    stratify=y,
)

print("Training data shape:", X_train.shape)
print("Test data shape:", X_test.shape)
print("Training target distribution:")
display(y_train.value_counts(normalize=True).sort_index().mul(100).round(2))
print("Test target distribution:")
display(y_test.value_counts(normalize=True).sort_index().mul(100).round(2))

Training data shape: (3228, 13)
Test data shape: (1384, 13)
Training target distribution:
proportion
status
0 70.14
1 29.86 

Test target distribution:
proportion
status
0 70.16
1 29.84

Train-Test Split Observations

  • The data was split into 3,228 training records and 1,384 test records.
  • Both training and test sets contain 13 features, meaning unnecessary columns such as ID were likely removed before modeling.
  • The target distribution is almost identical in both sets:
    • Training set: 70.14% class 0, 29.86% class 1
    • Test set: 70.16% class 0, 29.84% class 1
  • This shows that the split was likely stratified, preserving the original class balance.
  • The dataset still has a moderate class imbalance, so metrics such as precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC, and the confusion matrix should be considered in addition to accuracy.
  • Overall, the train-test split looks appropriate and reliable for model evaluation.

Data Pre-processing Pipeline¶

In [19]:
numeric_features = X.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns.tolist()
categorical_features = X.select_dtypes(include=["object", "category"]).columns.tolist()

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(
    transformers=[
        ("num", StandardScaler(), numeric_features),
        ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"), categorical_features),
    ],
    remainder="drop",
)

print("Numeric features:", numeric_features)
print("Categorical features:", categorical_features)

Numeric features: ['age', 'website_visits', 'time_spent_on_website', 'page_views_per_visit']
Categorical features: ['current_occupation', 'first_interaction', 'profile_completed', 'last_activity', 'print_media_type1', 'print_media_type2', 'digital_media', 'educational_channels', 'referral']

Feature Type Observations

  • There are 4 numeric features: age, website_visits, time_spent_on_website, and page_views_per_visit.
  • There are 9 categorical features, including occupation, interaction channel, activity type, media exposure, educational channels, and referral.
  • The target column status is correctly excluded from the feature lists.
  • The ID column is also excluded, which is appropriate because it is only a unique identifier and does not carry predictive meaning.
  • Numeric features should be scaled if models sensitive to feature magnitude are used, such as Logistic Regression, KNN, or SVM.
  • Categorical features need to be encoded before modeling, using methods such as one-hot encoding.
  • This separation is suitable for building a preprocessing pipeline with numeric and categorical transformations handled separately.

Model Building¶

Model Evaluation Criterion¶

The business objective is to identify leads that are likely to become paid customers so that ExtraaLearn can prioritize follow-up efforts. Missing a good lead may mean lost revenue, while marking too many weak leads as high priority can waste the sales team's time.

For this reason, F1-score is used as the primary model selection metric because it balances precision and recall for the converted class. Recall is also monitored closely because the company should avoid missing leads with a strong chance of conversion.

Define functions for Model Evaluation¶

In [20]:
# Function to compute different metrics to check performance of a classification model
def model_performance_classification(model, predictors, target):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check classifier model performance

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """

    pred = model.predict(predictors)

    acc = accuracy_score(target, pred)
    recall = recall_score(target, pred)
    precision = precision_score(target, pred)
    f1 = f1_score(target, pred)

    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Accuracy": acc, "Recall": recall, "Precision": precision, "F1": f1},
        index=[0],
    )

    return df_perf


def confusion_matrix_sklearn(model, predictors, target):
    """Plot a confusion matrix for a classification model."""
    pred = model.predict(predictors)
    cm = confusion_matrix(target, pred)
    plt.figure(figsize=(5, 4))
    sns.heatmap(
        cm,
        annot=True,
        fmt="d",
        cmap="Blues",
        xticklabels=["Not Converted", "Converted"],
        yticklabels=["Not Converted", "Converted"],
    )
    plt.ylabel("Actual")
    plt.xlabel("Predicted")
    plt.show()

Random Forest Model¶

In [21]:
rf_model = Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
        (
            "model",
            RandomForestClassifier(
                random_state=1,
                n_estimators=200,
                class_weight="balanced",
            ),
        ),
    ]
)

rf_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[21]:
Pipeline(steps=[('preprocessor',
                 ColumnTransformer(transformers=[('num', StandardScaler(),
                                                  ['age', 'website_visits',
                                                   'time_spent_on_website',
                                                   'page_views_per_visit']),
                                                 ('cat',
                                                  OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore'),
                                                  ['current_occupation',
                                                   'first_interaction',
                                                   'profile_completed',
                                                   'last_activity',
                                                   'print_media_type1',
                                                   'print_media_type2',
                                                   'digital_media',
                                                   'educational_channels',
                                                   'referral'])])),
                ('model',
                 RandomForestClassifier(class_weight='balanced',
                                        n_estimators=200, random_state=1))])

Checking model performance on training set¶

In [22]:
rf_train_perf = model_performance_classification(rf_model, X_train, y_train)
display(rf_train_perf)
confusion_matrix_sklearn(rf_model, X_train, y_train)
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.99938 1.0 0.99793 0.998964

Model Performance Observations

  • The model achieved an extremely high accuracy of 99.94%.
  • The recall is 1.00, meaning the model correctly identified all actual positive cases in the evaluated data.
  • The precision is 99.79%, meaning almost all predicted positive cases were truly positive.
  • The F1-score is 99.90%, showing an excellent balance between precision and recall.
  • These results suggest the model is performing almost perfectly.
  • However, the scores are unusually high, so it is important to check for possible data leakage, duplicate-like information, or preprocessing mistakes.
  • The model should also be validated on unseen test data or cross-validation results to confirm that the performance is genuine.

Checking model performance on test set¶

In [23]:
rf_test_perf = model_performance_classification(rf_model, X_test, y_test)
display(rf_test_perf)
confusion_matrix_sklearn(rf_model, X_test, y_test)
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.859104 0.723971 0.786842 0.754098

Model Performance Observations

  • The model achieved an accuracy of 85.91%, which is much better than the majority-class baseline of about 70%.
  • The recall is 72.40%, meaning the model correctly identifies about 72 out of every 100 actual positive cases.
  • The precision is 78.68%, meaning most predicted positives are truly positive.
  • The F1-score is 75.41%, showing a reasonable balance between precision and recall.
  • Compared with the earlier near-perfect result, this performance looks more realistic and less likely to be affected by leakage.
  • Since the dataset is moderately imbalanced, the F1-score and recall are more informative than accuracy alone.
  • Overall, the model performs well, but there is still room to improve recall if identifying positive cases is the main goal.

AdaBoost Classifier¶

In [24]:
adaboost_model = Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
        (
            "model",
            AdaBoostClassifier(
                estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1, random_state=1),
                random_state=1,
                n_estimators=100,
                learning_rate=0.1,
            ),
        ),
    ]
)

adaboost_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[24]:

                 ColumnTransformer(transformers=[('num', StandardScaler(),

                                                 ('cat',
                                                  OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore'),
                                                  ['current_occupation',

                                                   'profile_completed',

                                                                     random_state=1),
                                    learning_rate=0.1, n_estimators=100,
                                    random_state=1))])

Checking model performance on training set¶

In [25]:
adaboost_train_perf = model_performance_classification(adaboost_model, X_train, y_train)
display(adaboost_train_perf)
confusion_matrix_sklearn(adaboost_model, X_train, y_train)
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.810099 0.558091 0.742069 0.637063

Model Performance Observations

  • The model achieved an accuracy of 81.01%, which is above the majority-class baseline of about 70%.
  • The recall is 55.81%, meaning the model is missing a fairly large number of actual positive cases.
  • The precision is 74.21%, so when the model predicts a positive case, it is usually correct.
  • The F1-score is 63.71%, indicating a moderate balance between precision and recall.
  • Compared with the previous model, this model performs worse across all key metrics, especially recall.
  • The lower recall suggests the model is more conservative in predicting class 1.
  • If the business goal is to identify as many positive cases as possible, this model may not be the best choice.

Checking model performance on test set¶

In [26]:
adaboost_test_perf = model_performance_classification(adaboost_model, X_test, y_test)
display(adaboost_test_perf)
confusion_matrix_sklearn(adaboost_model, X_test, y_test)
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.825145 0.585956 0.773163 0.666667

Model Performance Observations

  • The model achieved an accuracy of 82.51%, which is better than the majority-class baseline of about 70%.
  • The recall is 58.60%, meaning the model identifies only a little over half of the actual positive cases.
  • The precision is 77.32%, showing that most predicted positive cases are correct.
  • The F1-score is 66.67%, indicating a moderate balance between precision and recall.
  • Compared with the previous model, this one performs slightly better in all metrics.
  • However, compared with the stronger model that had 85.91% accuracy and 75.41% F1-score, this model is weaker.
  • The model is still relatively conservative, as recall remains below 60%.
  • If capturing positive cases is important, this model may need threshold tuning, class balancing, or a stronger algorithm.

Model Performance Improvement - Hyperparameter Tuning¶

Hyperparameter Tuning - Random Forest¶

In [27]:
rf_tuned_model = Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
        ("model", RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight="balanced")),
    ]
)

rf_parameters = {
    "model__n_estimators": [100, 200],
    "model__max_depth": [4, 6, 8, None],
    "model__min_samples_split": [2, 5, 10],
    "model__min_samples_leaf": [1, 2, 4],
    "model__max_features": ["sqrt", None],
}

rf_grid = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=rf_tuned_model,
    param_grid=rf_parameters,
    scoring="f1",
    cv=5,
    n_jobs=-1,
)

rf_grid.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("Best parameters:", rf_grid.best_params_)
print("Best cross-validation F1-score:", rf_grid.best_score_)

tuned_rf_model = rf_grid.best_estimator_

Best parameters: {'model__max_depth': None, 'model__max_features': 'sqrt', 'model__min_samples_leaf': 2, 'model__min_samples_split': 10, 'model__n_estimators': 100}
Best cross-validation F1-score: 0.7797130130018683

Hyperparameter Tuning Observations

  • The best model used 100 estimators, meaning the random forest was built with 100 trees.
  • max_depth=None allows the trees to grow fully, which can capture complex patterns in the data.
  • max_features='sqrt' means each tree considers only a subset of features at each split, helping reduce overfitting.
  • min_samples_leaf=2 and min_samples_split=10 add some regularization, preventing the trees from splitting too aggressively.
  • The best cross-validation F1-score is 0.7797, or about 78.0%.
  • This suggests the tuned model performs well and balances precision and recall reasonably.
  • Since the tuned cross-validation F1-score is close to the earlier test F1-score, the model appears to generalize fairly well.
  • Overall, the tuned Random Forest is likely one of the stronger models for this dataset.

Checking model performance on training set¶

In [28]:
tuned_rf_train_perf = model_performance_classification(tuned_rf_model, X_train, y_train)
display(tuned_rf_train_perf)
confusion_matrix_sklearn(tuned_rf_model, X_train, y_train)
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.918216 0.917012 0.827715 0.870079

Tuned Model Performance Observations

  • The tuned model achieved a strong accuracy of 91.82%.
  • The recall is 91.70%, meaning it correctly identifies most actual positive cases.
  • The precision is 82.77%, so most predicted positive cases are truly positive.
  • The F1-score is 87.01%, showing a very good balance between precision and recall.
  • Compared with the earlier untuned models, this is a clear improvement, especially in recall and F1-score.
  • The high recall makes this model useful if the goal is to capture as many likely positive cases as possible.
  • Overall, the tuned model appears to be the best-performing model so far and generalizes well based on its test performance.

Checking model performance on test set¶

In [29]:
tuned_rf_test_perf = model_performance_classification(tuned_rf_model, X_test, y_test)
display(tuned_rf_test_perf)
confusion_matrix_sklearn(tuned_rf_model, X_test, y_test)
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.856936 0.791768 0.744875 0.767606

Model Performance Observations

  • The model achieved an accuracy of 85.69%, which is above the majority-class baseline of about 70%.
  • The recall is 79.18%, meaning it correctly identifies a good portion of actual positive cases.
  • The precision is 74.49%, so around three out of four predicted positives are truly positive.
  • The F1-score is 76.76%, showing a decent balance between precision and recall.
  • Compared with the tuned model with 91.82% accuracy and 87.01% F1-score, this model performs lower across all key metrics.
  • It still performs reasonably well, especially in recall, but it is not the strongest model so far.
  • If choosing between the evaluated models, the tuned model with the higher F1-score would be preferred.

Hyperparameter Tuning - AdaBoost Classifier¶

In [30]:
adaboost_tuned_model = Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
        ("model", AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)),
    ]
)

adaboost_parameters = {
    "model__estimator": [
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=2, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=1),
    ],
    "model__n_estimators": [50, 100, 150],
    "model__learning_rate": [0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0],
}

adaboost_grid = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=adaboost_tuned_model,
    param_grid=adaboost_parameters,
    scoring="f1",
    cv=5,
    n_jobs=-1,
)

adaboost_grid.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("Best parameters:", adaboost_grid.best_params_)
print("Best cross-validation F1-score:", adaboost_grid.best_score_)

tuned_adaboost_model = adaboost_grid.best_estimator_

Best parameters: {'model__estimator': DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=1), 'model__learning_rate': 0.5, 'model__n_estimators': 100}
Best cross-validation F1-score: 0.7648426197353182

Boosting Model Tuning Observations

  • The best boosting model used a Decision Tree with max_depth=3 as the base estimator.
  • It used 100 estimators, meaning 100 weak learners were combined.
  • The best learning_rate was 0.5, which controls how strongly each new learner contributes to the final model.
  • The best cross-validation F1-score is 0.7648, or about 76.5%.
  • This is a reasonable result, but it is lower than the tuned Random Forest cross-validation F1-score of about 78.0%.
  • The model may generalize fairly well, but based on cross-validation, it is not the strongest model so far.
  • Overall, the tuned boosting model performs well, but the tuned Random Forest still appears to be the better option.

Checking model performance on training set¶

In [31]:
tuned_adaboost_train_perf = model_performance_classification(tuned_adaboost_model, X_train, y_train)
display(tuned_adaboost_train_perf)
confusion_matrix_sklearn(tuned_adaboost_model, X_train, y_train)
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.86772 0.766598 0.785335 0.775853

Tuned Boosting Model Performance Observations

  • The tuned boosting model achieved an accuracy of 86.77%.
  • The recall is 76.66%, meaning it identifies about three-quarters of actual positive cases.
  • The precision is 78.53%, meaning most predicted positives are correct.
  • The F1-score is 77.59%, showing a balanced but moderate performance.
  • This test F1-score is close to its cross-validation F1-score of about 76.5%, so the model appears to generalize consistently.
  • However, it performs below the tuned Random Forest model, which achieved an F1-score of 87.01%.
  • Overall, the tuned boosting model is decent, but it is not the best model among the ones evaluated.

Checking model performance on test set¶

In [32]:
tuned_adaboost_test_perf = model_performance_classification(tuned_adaboost_model, X_test, y_test)
display(tuned_adaboost_test_perf)
confusion_matrix_sklearn(tuned_adaboost_model, X_test, y_test)
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.864162 0.757869 0.780549 0.769042

Model Performance Observations

  • The model achieved an accuracy of 86.42%.
  • The recall is 75.79%, meaning it correctly identifies about three-quarters of actual positive cases.
  • The precision is 78.05%, so most predicted positive cases are correct.
  • The F1-score is 76.90%, showing a reasonable balance between precision and recall.
  • This performance is very close to the tuned boosting model, but slightly lower across all metrics.
  • Compared with the best tuned Random Forest model, this model performs noticeably worse, especially in recall and F1-score.
  • Overall, it is a solid model, but not the strongest option based on the results so far.

Model Performance Comparison, Final Model Selection, and Serialization¶

In [33]:
models_train_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [
        rf_train_perf.T,
        adaboost_train_perf.T,
        tuned_rf_train_perf.T,
        tuned_adaboost_train_perf.T,
    ],
    axis=1,
)
models_train_comp_df.columns = [
    "Random Forest",
    "AdaBoost",
    "Tuned Random Forest",
    "Tuned AdaBoost",
]
print("Training performance comparison")
display(models_train_comp_df)

models_test_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [
        rf_test_perf.T,
        adaboost_test_perf.T,
        tuned_rf_test_perf.T,
        tuned_adaboost_test_perf.T,
    ],
    axis=1,
)
models_test_comp_df.columns = [
    "Random Forest",
    "AdaBoost",
    "Tuned Random Forest",
    "Tuned AdaBoost",
]
print("Test performance comparison")
display(models_test_comp_df)

if tuned_adaboost_test_perf.loc[0, "F1"] >= tuned_rf_test_perf.loc[0, "F1"]:
    final_model = tuned_adaboost_model
    final_model_name = "Tuned AdaBoost"
else:
    final_model = tuned_rf_model
    final_model_name = "Tuned Random Forest"

print("Final selected model:", final_model_name)

try:
    feature_names = final_model.named_steps["preprocessor"].get_feature_names_out()
    importances = final_model.named_steps["model"].feature_importances_
    importance_df = (
        pd.DataFrame({"Feature": feature_names, "Importance": importances})
        .sort_values("Importance", ascending=False)
        .head(20)
    )
    display(importance_df)
except Exception as exc:
    print("Feature importance could not be generated:", exc)

os.makedirs("backend_files", exist_ok=True)

model_bundle = {
    "model": final_model,
    "features": X.columns.tolist(),
    "categorical_features": categorical_features,
    "numeric_features": numeric_features,
    "target": "status",
    "model_name": final_model_name,
}

joblib.dump(model_bundle, "model.joblib")
joblib.dump(model_bundle, "backend_files/model.joblib")
print("Model serialized as model.joblib and backend_files/model.joblib")

Training performance comparison
Random Forest AdaBoost Tuned Random Forest Tuned AdaBoost
Accuracy 0.999380 0.810099 0.918216 0.867720
Recall 1.000000 0.558091 0.917012 0.766598
Precision 0.997930 0.742069 0.827715 0.785335
F1 0.998964 0.637063 0.870079 0.775853 
Test performance comparison
Random Forest AdaBoost Tuned Random Forest Tuned AdaBoost
Accuracy 0.859104 0.825145 0.856936 0.864162
Recall 0.723971 0.585956 0.791768 0.757869
Precision 0.786842 0.773163 0.744875 0.780549
F1 0.754098 0.666667 0.767606 0.769042 
Final selected model: Tuned AdaBoost
Feature Importance
2 num__time_spent_on_website 3.084545e-01
7 cat__first_interaction_Mobile App 1.473394e-01
8 cat__first_interaction_Website 9.760048e-02
0 num__age 7.118904e-02
11 cat__profile_completed_Medium 6.044096e-02
1 num__website_visits 5.480122e-02
9 cat__profile_completed_High 5.196221e-02
3 num__page_views_per_visit 5.066605e-02
4 cat__current_occupation_Professional 3.555130e-02
10 cat__profile_completed_Low 2.333200e-02
23 cat__referral_No 1.819995e-02
14 cat__last_activity_Website Activity 1.770110e-02
13 cat__last_activity_Phone Activity 1.735953e-02
24 cat__referral_Yes 1.699624e-02
6 cat__current_occupation_Unemployed 1.505791e-02
12 cat__last_activity_Email Activity 9.706302e-03
15 cat__print_media_type1_No 3.186340e-03
22 cat__educational_channels_Yes 4.554523e-04
21 cat__educational_channels_No 1.982797e-16
17 cat__print_media_type2_No 6.690757e-17 
Model serialized as model.joblib and backend_files/model.joblib

Final Model Comparison Observations

  • The original Random Forest performed almost perfectly on training data, with an F1-score of 0.999, but dropped to 0.754 on test data. This suggests overfitting.
  • AdaBoost had the weakest performance overall, especially with test recall of 0.586 and F1-score of 0.667.
  • Tuned Random Forest improved recall on the test set to 0.792, making it better at identifying actual positive cases.
  • Tuned AdaBoost achieved the best overall test performance, with:
    • Accuracy: 86.42%
    • Recall: 75.79%
    • Precision: 78.05%
    • F1-score: 76.90%
  • Tuned AdaBoost has the highest test F1-score, but only slightly higher than Tuned Random Forest. So the final choice is reasonable, especially if balanced performance and precision are important.
  • If the main business goal is to capture as many positive leads as possible, Tuned Random Forest could also be considered because it has higher recall.

Feature Importance Observations

  • time_spent_on_website is the most important predictor, contributing about 30.8% importance.
  • first_interaction is highly influential, especially whether the user first interacted through the Mobile App or Website.
  • age, profile_completed, website_visits, and page_views_per_visit also contribute meaningfully to prediction.
  • Profile completion matters: users with different completion levels show different likelihoods of conversion.
  • Referral has some predictive value, but its importance is lower than expected, likely because referral users are a very small group.
  • Print media, digital media, and educational channel features have very low importance, suggesting they add little predictive value to the final model.

Overall, the Tuned AdaBoost model is a strong final choice, with time_spent_on_website, first_interaction, and profile_completed emerging as the most important drivers of conversion.

Deployment - Backend¶

Flask Web Framework¶

In [34]:
%%writefile backend_files/app.py
from pathlib import Path

import joblib
import pandas as pd
from flask import Flask, jsonify, request


MODEL_PATH = Path(__file__).with_name("model.joblib")
MODEL_BUNDLE = joblib.load(MODEL_PATH)
MODEL = MODEL_BUNDLE["model"]
FEATURES = MODEL_BUNDLE["features"]

app = Flask(__name__)


def _payload_to_frame(payload):
    if isinstance(payload, dict):
        records = payload.get("data", payload)
    elif isinstance(payload, list):
        records = payload
    else:
        raise ValueError("Payload must be a JSON object or a list of objects.")

    if isinstance(records, dict):
        records = [records]

    frame = pd.DataFrame(records)
    missing = [column for column in FEATURES if column not in frame.columns]
    if missing:
        raise ValueError(f"Missing required field(s): {', '.join(missing)}")

    return frame[FEATURES]


@app.get("/")
def health_check():
    return jsonify(
        {
            "status": "ok",
            "model": MODEL_BUNDLE.get("model_name", "ExtraaLearn lead conversion model"),
            "features": FEATURES,
        }
    )


@app.post("/predict")
def predict():
    try:
        payload = request.get_json(force=True)
        input_data = _payload_to_frame(payload)
        predictions = MODEL.predict(input_data).astype(int).tolist()

        if hasattr(MODEL, "predict_proba"):
            probabilities = MODEL.predict_proba(input_data)[:, 1].round(4).tolist()
        else:
            probabilities = [None] * len(predictions)

        results = [
            {
                "prediction": prediction,
                "prediction_label": "Converted" if prediction == 1 else "Not Converted",
                "conversion_probability": probability,
            }
            for prediction, probability in zip(predictions, probabilities)
        ]
        return jsonify({"results": results})
    except Exception as exc:
        return jsonify({"error": str(exc)}), 400


if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=7860)

Writing backend_files/app.py

COPY . .

RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

EXPOSE 7860

CMD ["python", "app.py"]

    "categorical_features": categorical_features,
    "numeric_features": numeric_features,
    "target": "status",
    "model_name": final_model_name,
}

joblib.dump(model_bundle, "backend_files/model.joblib")

Model saved: True

In [38]:
import os
import shutil

os.makedirs("backend_files", exist_ok=True)

# If model.joblib exists in the main Colab folder, copy it into backend_files
if os.path.exists("model.joblib"):
    shutil.copy("model.joblib", "backend_files/model.joblib")

print(os.listdir("backend_files"))
print("Model exists:", os.path.exists("backend_files/model.joblib"))

['Dockerfile', 'model.joblib', 'requirements.txt', 'app.py']
Model exists: True

Setting up a Hugging Face Docker Space for the Backend¶

Note: We are creating a Hugging Face Docker Space for our backend using the Hugging Face Hub API. This automates the space creation process and enables seamless deployment of our Flask app.

In [46]:
# Authenticate with Hugging Face.
import os
from huggingface_hub import login, HfApi
from google.colab import userdata
hf_token = userdata.get("HF_TOKEN")

try:
    from google.colab import userdata
    hf_token = hf_token or userdata.get("HF_TOKEN")
except Exception:
    pass

if not hf_token:
    raise ValueError("Please set HF_TOKEN before creating or uploading to Hugging Face Spaces.")

login(token=hf_token)
api = HfApi()

me = api.whoami(token=hf_token)

Note : If you were trying with different names, be cautious when using a underscore _ in space names, such as frontend_space, as it can cause exceptions when accessing the API. Always use an hyphen - instead, like frontend-space.

In [40]:
HF_USERNAME = me["name"]
BACKEND_SPACE_NAME = "extraalearn-backend"
backend_space_id = f"{HF_USERNAME}/{BACKEND_SPACE_NAME}"

api.create_repo(
    repo_id=backend_space_id,
    repo_type="space",
    space_sdk="docker",
    exist_ok=True,
    private=False,
)

print("Backend Space:", backend_space_id)
print("Backend API URL:", f"https://{HF_USERNAME}-{BACKEND_SPACE_NAME}.hf.space")

Backend Space: omohemma/extraalearn-backend
Backend API URL: https://omohemma-extraalearn-backend.hf.space

Uploading Files to Hugging Face Space (Docker Space)¶

Note: Before running the code below, ensure that the serialized ML model has been uploaded in to backend_files folder.

In [41]:
# Ensure backend_files contains app.py, requirements.txt, Dockerfile, and model.joblib before running this cell.
api.upload_folder(
    folder_path="backend_files",
    repo_id=backend_space_id,
    repo_type="space",
)

print("Backend files uploaded successfully.")

Backend files uploaded successfully.

Deployment - Frontend¶

Points to note before executing the below cells¶

  • Create a Streamlit space on Hugging Face by following the instructions provided on the content page titled Creating Spaces and Adding Secrets in Hugging Face from Week 1

Streamlit for Interactive UI¶

In [54]:
%%writefile frontend_files/app.py
import os

import pandas as pd
import requests
import streamlit as st


DEFAULT_BACKEND_URL = os.getenv("BACKEND_URL", "").rstrip("/")

st.set_page_config(page_title="ExtraaLearn Lead Conversion", page_icon="EL", layout="centered")
st.title("ExtraaLearn Lead Conversion")

backend_url = st.sidebar.text_input("Backend URL", value=DEFAULT_BACKEND_URL)

tab_single, tab_batch = st.tabs(["Single Lead", "Batch Scoring"])

with tab_single:
    age = st.number_input("Age", min_value=18, max_value=80, value=35, step=1)
    current_occupation = st.selectbox("Current Occupation", ["Professional", "Unemployed", "Student"])
    first_interaction = st.selectbox("First Interaction", ["Website", "Mobile App"])
    profile_completed = st.selectbox("Profile Completed", ["Low", "Medium", "High"], index=2)
    website_visits = st.number_input("Website Visits", min_value=0, max_value=50, value=5, step=1)
    time_spent_on_website = st.number_input(
        "Time Spent on Website", min_value=0, max_value=5000, value=700, step=10
    )
    page_views_per_visit = st.number_input(
        "Page Views per Visit", min_value=0.0, max_value=25.0, value=3.0, step=0.1
    )
    last_activity = st.selectbox(
        "Last Activity", ["Website Activity", "Email Activity", "Phone Activity"]
    )
    print_media_type1 = st.selectbox("Newspaper Ad", ["No", "Yes"])
    print_media_type2 = st.selectbox("Magazine Ad", ["No", "Yes"])
    digital_media = st.selectbox("Digital Media Ad", ["No", "Yes"])
    educational_channels = st.selectbox("Educational Channels", ["No", "Yes"])
    referral = st.selectbox("Referral", ["No", "Yes"])

    payload = {
        "age": age,
        "current_occupation": current_occupation,
        "first_interaction": first_interaction,
        "profile_completed": profile_completed,
        "website_visits": website_visits,
        "time_spent_on_website": time_spent_on_website,
        "page_views_per_visit": page_views_per_visit,
        "last_activity": last_activity,
        "print_media_type1": print_media_type1,
        "print_media_type2": print_media_type2,
        "digital_media": digital_media,
        "educational_channels": educational_channels,
        "referral": referral,
    }

    if st.button("Predict Conversion", type="primary"):
        if not backend_url:
            st.error("Backend URL is required.")
        else:
            response = requests.post(f"{backend_url}/predict", json=payload, timeout=30)
            if response.ok:
                result = response.json()["results"][0]
                probability = result.get("conversion_probability")
                st.metric("Prediction", result["prediction_label"])
                if probability is not None:
                    st.metric("Conversion Probability", f"{probability:.1%}")
            else:
                st.error(response.json().get("error", response.text))

with tab_batch:
    uploaded_file = st.file_uploader("Upload Leads CSV", type=["csv"])
    if uploaded_file is not None:
        batch_data = pd.read_csv(uploaded_file)
        st.dataframe(batch_data.head(), use_container_width=True)

        if st.button("Score Batch"):
            if not backend_url:
                st.error("Backend URL is required.")
            else:
                records = batch_data.to_dict(orient="records")
                response = requests.post(f"{backend_url}/predict", json={"data": records}, timeout=60)
                if response.ok:
                    results = pd.DataFrame(response.json()["results"])
                    scored_data = pd.concat([batch_data.reset_index(drop=True), results], axis=1)
                    st.dataframe(scored_data, use_container_width=True)
                    st.download_button(
                        "Download Scores",
                        scored_data.to_csv(index=False).encode("utf-8"),
                        "extraalearn_predictions.csv",
                        "text/csv",
                    )
                else:
                    st.error(response.json().get("error", response.text))

Overwriting frontend_files/app.py

Uploading Files to Hugging Face Space (Streamlit Space)¶

In [59]:
# Create a Streamlit Space manually on Hugging Face or use the code below.
# Add BACKEND_URL as a Space secret, for example:
# https://your-huggingface-username-extraalearn-backend.hf.space

FRONTEND_SPACE_NAME = "extraalearn-frontend"
frontend_space_id = f"{HF_USERNAME}/{FRONTEND_SPACE_NAME}"

api.create_repo(
    repo_id=frontend_space_id,
    repo_type="space",
    space_sdk="docker",
    exist_ok=True,
    private=False,
)

api.upload_folder(
    folder_path="frontend_files",
    repo_id=frontend_space_id,
    repo_type="space",
)

print("Frontend files uploaded successfully.")
print("Frontend Space:", frontend_space_id)
FRONTEND_SPACE_NAME = "extraalearn-frontend"
print("Frontend URL:", f"https://{HF_USERNAME}-{FRONTEND_SPACE_NAME}.hf.space")

No files have been modified since last commit. Skipping to prevent empty commit.
WARNING:huggingface_hub.hf_api:No files have been modified since last commit. Skipping to prevent empty commit.

Frontend files uploaded successfully.
Frontend Space: omohemma/extraalearn-frontend
Frontend URL: https://omohemma-extraalearn-frontend.hf.space

Actionable Insights and Business Recommendations¶

  • The dataset contains 4,612 leads, with about 29.86% converting to paid customers. This shows that conversion is not evenly distributed, so lead prioritization is important.

  • Leads whose first interaction was through the Website had a much higher conversion rate than leads from the Mobile App. ExtraaLearn should strengthen website-based campaigns and study why mobile app leads convert less.

  • Leads with High profile completion were more likely to convert. The company should encourage users to complete their profiles through reminders, progress prompts, and simplified onboarding.

  • Referral leads showed the strongest conversion behavior. ExtraaLearn should invest more in referral programs, incentives, and quick follow-up for referred leads.

  • Converted leads spent more time on the website than non-converted leads. Time spent on website should be treated as a strong buying-intent signal for sales prioritization.

  • The final selected model can help the sales team rank leads based on conversion probability, allowing representatives to focus first on leads with the highest likelihood of conversion.

  • ExtraaLearn should use batch scoring regularly to refresh lead priority lists and monitor model performance over time as customer behavior changes.

Power Ahead
